WEN.title: McDonald's Stock Hits 52-Week Lows: High Gas Prices Could Make Things Worse

macro_events.event_summary: April 2026 inflation print came in hot, pressuring Fed Chair Warsh to hike rates rather than cut.
primary_drivers: Higher-for-longer / rising short-end rates lift NIM for asset-sensitive lenders
Higher discount rate compresses long-duration equity valuations
Persistent inflation supports real assets and pricing-power names; hurts rate-sensitive housing/REITs

beneficiaries.rationale: Highly asset-sensitive: client cash sweep yields and treasury reinvestment rates rise with short rates; previously hurt by deposit flight at higher rates but now benefits from steeper curve and reinvestment of low-yielding portfolio.
Largest US bank with positive asset sensitivity disclosed in 10-Q; +100bp parallel shift historically adds ~$2-3B to NII. Hot CPI removes cut expectations, supporting net interest margin.
Interest rate futures/options (SOFR, Treasury) volumes spike on repricing of Fed path; CME's rate complex is ~40% of ADV and directly benefits from hike repricing.
Life insurers reinvest at higher yields; rising rates expand spread on annuity and GA portfolios. Manulife and peers (MET, PRU) are classic rate-up trades.
Energy is a key driver of any April inflation spike; integrated oils benefit from sustained crude/refining margins that are feeding the CPI print itself.

losers.rationale: Homebuilders are the most rate-sensitive equity group; mortgage rates re-spike on hike pricing, killing affordability and order growth. D.R. Horton is the largest pure-play.
Realty Income and triple-net REITs trade as bond proxies with high duration; cap rates widen with the 10Y, and dividend yield must reprice higher (price down).
Rocket Companies is levered to mortgage refi/origination volumes which collapse when rates back up; revenue is near-direct function of mortgage rate level.
Auto demand is rate-sensitive (financing) and Tesla's valuation has long-duration component; double hit from financing demand destruction and discount rate.

headlines: April’s inflation spike leaves Warsh and the Fed zero excuses not to raise rates

macro_events.event_summary: CNBC highlights bullish options/equity positioning in China-exposed US stocks coinciding with a Trump visit to China, suggesting traders are betting on de-escalation or trade deal optimism.
primary_drivers: Headline-driven sentiment around potential US-China trade détente
Re-rating of China-revenue-exposed US equities on lower tariff/geopolitical risk premium
Short-covering and options flow in names with heavy China revenue mix

beneficiaries.rationale: Wynn Resorts derives ~70% of EBITDA from Macau; any thaw in US-China relations directly de-risks Macau license/operating environment and visa/travel flows. Classic pure-play China-headline trade.
Las Vegas Sands is essentially a Macau/Singapore pure-play after divesting US assets; ~60%+ of EBITDA from Macau makes it the second canonical 'Trump-visits-China' long.
Shanghai Gigafactory accounts for ~40% of global Tesla deliveries; China sales and regulatory tailwinds (FSD approval, tariff relief on battery inputs) are highly sensitive to bilateral tone.
Caterpillar has ~5-10% direct China revenue plus heavy exposure to global commodity demand tied to Chinese construction; serves as a cyclical proxy for China reflation hopes.
Boeing is a recurring 'trade deal deliverable' — China aircraft orders are historically announced around bilateral summits. Visit creates optionality on a large 737/787 order headline.

losers.rationale: Defense primes can de-rate on US-China détente headlines as the geopolitical risk premium compresses; Lockheed is the most Pacific-theater-levered prime via F-35 and missile defense.
Palantir's narrative premium partly rests on great-power competition; softening US-China rhetoric can compress its geopolitical-AI multiple at the margin.

headlines: Bulls bet big on these three China-related stock trades during Trump visit

macro_events.event_summary: A MarketWatch piece argues global oil supply has dropped by nearly 1 billion barrels over the past 75 days and that markets are underpricing the tightening, suggesting upside risk to crude prices.
primary_drivers: Tightening physical crude balance / inventory drawdown supports higher spot and forward oil prices
Higher realized prices flow directly to upstream E&P revenue and margins; refiners face higher feedstock costs
Fuel cost passthrough pressures transportation-heavy consumers (airlines, trucking)

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with large upstream exposure (Permian, Guyana); every $5/bbl move in Brent meaningfully lifts upstream EBITDA. Direct leverage to a supply-driven price rally.
Upstream-heavy integrated; Permian and TCO production give direct realized-price leverage. Trades as a high-beta proxy to crude among megacaps.
Pure-play Permian E&P with high oil cut (~70%+ liquids); among the highest cash-flow torque per dollar of crude move in the large-cap E&P group.
Low-cost, oil-weighted shale producer; FCF and buyback capacity scale directly with WTI. Historically one of the cleanest E&P beta plays to crude.
Highly oil-levered balance sheet (post-Anadarko/CrownRock); shares are among the most crude-sensitive large caps and benefit disproportionately from supply tightness narratives.
Global oilfield services leader — sustained higher prices reinforce operator capex plans internationally, where SLB has the largest footprint. Second-order but well-established linkage.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~20-25% of operating costs; Delta has limited hedging vs peers, so a sustained crude move directly compresses unit margins. Highly crude-sensitive in sell-side models.
Similar fuel cost exposure as DAL with minimal hedging program; unit cost (CASM-ex) guidance is directly threatened by sustained higher crude.
Southwest is a single-fleet domestic carrier where fuel is the largest non-labor cost; partially hedged but still meaningfully exposed, and lacks international premium-cabin offset.
Refiners face higher feedstock (crude) costs; while crack spreads can offset, a supply-shock-driven crude rally typically compresses refining margins before products catch up.
Merchant refiner with no upstream offset — purely long crack spreads. Crude-led rallies (vs product-led) typically pressure refining margins near-term.
LTL trucking with diesel as a major variable cost; fuel surcharges lag and during freight-recession conditions, full passthrough is harder to achieve.

headlines: The world lost nearly 1 billion barrels in oil supply over the past 75 days. Why investors aren’t worrying enough.
